1: The sin of Judah is written with a pen of iron; with a point of a diamond it is engraved on the tablet of their heart, and on the horns of their altars. Chapter 17 opens with an indelibility metaphor. The iron stylus (et barzel) and diamond point (tsiporen shamir) are the hardest available writing instruments — used to inscribe on rock, not parchment. The sin of Judah is not a light smudge easily erased but a deep incision on the tablet of their heart — an ironic inversion of Proverbs 3:3 (write these things on the tablet of your heart) and anticipating the New Covenant of Jeremiah 31:33 (I will write my law on their hearts). The altars bear the same inscription: their worship system itself is marked with sin.
2: While their children remember their altars and their Asherah poles, beside every green tree and on the high hills. The intergenerational transmission of idolatry: their children remember their altars — the children who grow up watching parents worship at the Asherah poles and high places inherit a religious memory oriented away from YHWH. The green tree and high hill are the standard markers of Canaanite sacred sites throughout the Deuteronomistic History. The landscape of Judah has been spiritually colonized by these sites, and children are being shaped by their geography.
3: O my mountain in the open country — your wealth and all your treasures I will give for spoil as the price of your sin throughout all your territory. The address shifts to Judah as YHWH's mountain-possession — once his treasure, now plunder. The wealth and treasures will be taken by Babylon as the covenant price of Judah's sin. The accumulated material blessing of YHWH's covenant faithfulness (Deuteronomy 28:3–8) is transferred to enemies as the accumulated material curse of covenant violation (Deuteronomy 28:43–44).
4: You shall loosen your hand from your heritage that I gave to you, and I will make you serve your enemies in a land that you do not know, for in my anger a fire is kindled that shall burn forever. The loss of the covenant land is stated as a compelled act: Judah will loosen her hand from the inheritance YHWH gave — a legal image of releasing a property claim. The exile will mean serving enemies in an unknown land (cf. Jeremiah 15:14). The fire burning forever reiterates the hyperbolic severity of covenant judgment that will not be easily or quickly reversed.
5: Thus says YHWH: Cursed is the man who trusts in man and makes flesh his strength, whose heart turns away from YHWH. The wisdom section (vv5–8) presents a classic two-ways contrast that parallels Psalm 1 closely. The curse (arur) falls on the one who trusts in basar (flesh) — specifically, human military and political alliances. In Jeremiah's context this is the policy debate: should Judah trust Egypt and other human allies against Babylon, or submit to YHWH's judgment? Those who counsel trust in human power are cursed because their heart turns from YHWH — the fundamental apostasy expressed as foreign policy.
6: He is like a shrub in the desert, and shall not see any good come. He shall dwell in the parched places of the wilderness, in an uninhabited salt land. The one who trusts in human strength is like a desert shrub — stunted, unable to access water, existing in barren isolation. The salt land (eretz melechah) is completely infertile. Against the lush imagery of the blessed man (v8), the cursed man's image is stark: he has chosen the most inhospitable environment possible by trusting in what cannot sustain life.
7: Blessed is the man who trusts in YHWH, whose trust is YHWH. The blessing (baruk) is the counterpart of v5's curse. The double expression trusts in YHWH, whose trust is YHWH is emphatic — YHWH himself is the trust, not merely the object of trust. This matches the vocabulary of the lament in Jeremiah 14:8 (hope of Israel, mikveh yisrael) — YHWH as the reservoir toward which trust flows. The Psalm 1 parallel is clear: the blessed man's alternative is not merely different behavior but a different relational grounding.
8: He is like a tree planted by water, that sends out its roots by the stream, and does not fear when heat comes, for its leaves remain green, and is not anxious in the year of drought, for it does not cease to bear fruit. The image of the tree planted by streams appears in Psalms 1:3 with nearly identical language, suggesting a shared wisdom tradition. The tree's key feature is its connection to a water source that is independent of surface rainfall: even in drought (shanah batzoret — the same word as the drought of Jeremiah 14), the deep-rooted tree has access to underground water and keeps bearing fruit. Trust in YHWH provides access to resources inaccessible to the flesh-trusting shrub.
9: The heart is deceitful above all things, and desperately sick; who can understand it? One of Scripture's most cited verses on human anthropology. The Hebrew aqov halev mikol umiyashenu — the heart is more crooked than all things, and desperately weak/sick. Aqov (crooked, deceitful) shares a root with Jacob (ya'aqov) — the heel-grasper, the supplanter. The heart has the Jacob-quality of crooked self-interest. Anush (desperately sick) is terminal illness language. The rhetorical question — who can understand it? — is not rhetorical despair but preparation for the answer in v10: YHWH alone can assess the heart.
10: I the LORD search the heart and test the mind, to give every man according to his ways, according to the fruit of his deeds. YHWH's answer to the unknowable heart: he is the one who searches (choqer lev, examines the heart) and tests (bachen kelayot, tries the kidneys — the seat of deep emotion). This is the basis for covenant judgment that is truly just: human judges cannot see the inner life; YHWH can. The rendering according to his ways, according to the fruit of his deeds is covenant recompense — what a person sows in their inner and outer life, they reap in consequence.
11: Like the partridge that gathers a brood that she did not hatch, so is he who gets riches but not by justice; in the mid of his days they will leave him, and at his end he will be a fool. The partridge proverb illustrates the impermanence of unjustly acquired wealth. The partridge was believed to steal other birds' eggs and raise them as her own — but the chicks eventually recognize their true nature and leave. The wealth gathered by injustice has the same quality: it doesn't belong to the one who holds it, and it will depart. In the context of Jerusalem's impending collapse, the wealthy who have accumulated through exploitation will lose everything.
12: A glorious throne set on high from the beginning is the place of our sanctuary. A brief doxological interjection: YHWH's throne in the Temple is exalted from the beginning (merom merishon makom miqdashenu). The contrast with the crooked human heart (v9) and unjust wealth (v11) is the transcendent stability of YHWH's throne. The Temple as the location of divine sovereignty provides the anchor for Jeremiah's covenant theology: even amid judgment, the divine throne remains exalted.
13: O YHWH, the hope of Israel, all who forsake you shall be put to shame; those who turn away from you shall be written in the earth, for they have forsaken YHWH, the fountain of living waters. The hope of Israel (mikveh yisrael) recurs from Jeremiah 14:8. Written in the earth (beyeretzu yikatevu) contrasts with the heavenly register (the Book of Life concept): those who forsake YHWH are inscribed in dust, not in permanent record — their existence is temporary and their fate is oblivion. Fountain of living waters (meqor mayim chayyim) reappears from Jeremiah 2:13 — YHWH as the source of life-giving water which the people have abandoned for leaking cisterns.
14: Heal me, O YHWH, and I shall be healed; save me, and I shall be saved, for you are my praise. Jeremiah's personal lament resumes. The double emphasis — heal me… and I shall be healed; save me… and I shall be saved — insists that divine action is the only effective source of restoration. Human remedies for spiritual and physical illness are insufficient; YHWH's action alone heals completely. You are my praise (tehilati attah) identifies YHWH as the only proper object of Jeremiah's testimony — his whole life is a witness to YHWH's character.
15: Behold, they say to me, 'Where is the word of YHWH? Let it come!' The mockers challenge Jeremiah's prophetic credibility: years of announcing judgment without visible fulfillment have made his predictions seem empty. The challenge let it come is sarcastic — they are daring the judgment to arrive. This is the standard test for prophetic authenticity in Deuteronomy 18:21–22: if the predicted event does not occur, the prophet is false. The people are using the delay of Babylon's arrival as evidence against Jeremiah.
16: I have not run away from being your shepherd, nor have I desired the day of disaster. You know what came out of my lips; it was before your face. Jeremiah defends the sincerity of his prophetic ministry. He has not run from being shepherd (some translate hastened the day of disaster) — he did not relish announcing doom. You know what came out of my lips: the prophet's words are fully transparent before YHWH, who can verify that Jeremiah has spoken only what he was given, not added personal hostility to the divine message.
17: Be not a terror to me; you are my refuge in the day of evil. The petition is directed at YHWH himself — the prophet who fears his persecutors prays that YHWH will not be an additional source of terror. You are my refuge (machsi attah) — a Psalmic assertion of divine protection (Psalms 46:1; Jeremiah 91:2) now placed on Jeremiah's lips. Even while accusing YHWH of being like a failing wadi (Jeremiah 15:18), Jeremiah continues to pray to YHWH as his refuge. The tension is authentic rather than resolved.
18: Let those be put to shame who persecute me, but let me not be put to shame; let them be dismayed, but let me not be dismayed; bring upon them the day of evil; destroy them with double destruction! The imprecatory close of Jeremiah's lament. The prayer for double destruction (mishne shivron) on his persecutors mirrors the double-repayment vocabulary of covenant law (v18 of Jeremiah 16). Jeremiah is not calling for personal revenge but for covenant enforcement: those who persecute YHWH's messenger should receive what they have attempted to bring on the messenger. The imprecatory psalms and prophetic prayers are covenant prayers, not personal vindictiveness.
19: Thus said YHWH to me: Go and stand in the People's Gate, by which the kings of Judah enter and by which they go out, and in all the gates of Jerusalem. The final section of chapter 17 shifts to a specific public mission: Jeremiah is to preach Sabbath observance at the city gates. The People's Gate (possibly the gate through which the general public entered, as opposed to a royal or priestly entrance) and all the gates of Jerusalem ensure maximum public exposure. The choice of gates as pulpit is significant: gates were the nerve centers of civic life — commerce, legal proceedings, military passage all occurred there.
20: And say to them: Hear the word of YHWH, you kings of Judah, and all Judah, and all the inhabitants of Jerusalem who enter by these gates. The audience is comprehensive: kings, all Judah, all Jerusalem. The Sabbath-gate sermon addresses every stratum of society. The king's obedience or disobedience to Sabbath is as relevant as the commoner's — there is no social exemption from covenant obligations. The inclusion of kings (plural) may reflect the political instability of Jeremiah's period, with multiple short-reigned kings.
21: Thus says YHWH: Take care for the sake of your lives, and do not bear a burden on the Sabbath day or bring it in by the gates of Jerusalem. Sabbath commerce — carrying goods through the city gates on the seventh day — is the specific violation Jeremiah targets. The Sabbath was an economic day of rest: no buying, selling, or trade activity. In a market-city like Jerusalem, where the gates were the commercial entry-points, Sabbath observance required deliberate suspension of the economic rhythms that defined daily life. The phrase for the sake of your lives (bishmarot nafshoteikhem) makes Sabbath-keeping a survival issue, not merely a piety requirement.
22: And do not carry a burden out of your houses on the Sabbath or do any work, but keep the Sabbath day holy, as I commanded your fathers. The Sabbath command is grounded in the original Decalogue obligation (Exodus 20:8–11; Deuteronomy 5:12–15) — the ancestral fathers were commanded to keep it holy. The present generation is not being given new legislation but called back to foundational covenant practice. The work-prohibition extends from the gates (public commerce) to the houses (private labor), covering the full range of economic activity.
23: Yet they did not listen or incline their ear, but stiffened their neck, that they might not hear and receive instruction. The historical record is entered as evidence: Judah's ancestors received the same Sabbath command and refused it. Stiffened their neck (hikshachafam) — the resistance is physical, deliberate, embodied. The neck metaphor describes the stubborn animal that refuses the yoke — covenant as yoke, refusal as stiff-necked rebellion. Exodus 32:9 uses the same image for the golden calf generation.
24: But if you listen to me, declares YHWH, and bring in no burden by the gates of this city on the Sabbath day, but keep the Sabbath day holy and do no work on it. The conditional restoration formula: if you listen (im shamo'a tishme'un — the emphatic infinitive absolute + finite verb). The Sabbath is presented as a test case for the fundamental covenant question of hearing and obeying YHWH. It is a weekly, visible, measurable act of covenant faithfulness — or unfaithfulness. The Sabbath thus functions as a kind of covenant thermometer.
25: Then there shall enter by the gates of this city kings and princes who sit on the throne of David, riding in chariots and on horses, they and their officials, the men of Judah and the inhabitants of Jerusalem. And this city shall be inhabited forever. The restoration promise is remarkable in its specificity: Sabbath observance will result in stable Davidic kingship and permanent city habitation. The promise connects Sabbath to the entire covenant-blessing complex: political stability (kings and princes on David's throne), military honor (chariots and horses), permanent inhabitation. The link between Sabbath and national flourishing is direct — not magical but covenantal.
26: And people shall come from the cities of Judah and the places around Jerusalem, from the land of Benjamin, from the Shephelah, from the hill country, and from the Negeb, bringing burnt offerings and sacrifices, grain offerings and frankincense, and bringing thank offerings to the house of YHWH. The geographic comprehensiveness of the restoration: the full tribal territory of Judah — Shephelah (lowlands), hill country, Negeb (south), Benjamin (north) — will be represented at the Temple. The sacrificial system will function as designed: worship from every corner of the covenant land flowing to the Jerusalem sanctuary. The picture is of a fully restored covenant community.
27: But if you do not listen to me, to keep the Sabbath day holy, and not to bear a burden and enter by the gates of Jerusalem on the Sabbath day, then I will kindle a fire in its gates, and it shall devour the palaces of Jerusalem and shall not be quenched. The threat closes the chapter with covenant symmetry: the same gates through which Sabbath-violating commerce flows will be consumed by fire. The palaces of Jerusalem — symbols of royal power and wealth — will be incinerated. Shall not be quenched echoes the unquenchable fire of v4 and Jeremiah 15:14. The fire of divine judgment, once kindled by accumulated covenant violation, cannot be extinguished by human effort — only by repentance and covenant return, the alternative offered in the preceding verses.
